body:
What if the space between your dreams, the static-filled void you dismiss as nothing, is actually the most potent spiritual classroom you’ll ever enter? What if the territory you traverse each night is not just a replay of your day, but a direct, unmediated encounter with the architecture of your own consciousness, and a rehearsal for the most significant journey you will ever take?

We have been taught to fear the unknown, to pathologize the void. We fill our waking lives with noise and distraction to avoid the deep, resonant silence that holds the truth of our being. In the dreamtime, this avoidance is stripped away. You are left with the raw, unfiltered reality of your own mind. This is not a place for the faint of heart. It is a place for warriors of consciousness, for those willing to look into the abyss and not flinch. The modern world, with its obsession with productivity and material gain, has rendered us spiritually illiterate, especially when it comes to the nocturnal landscape. We are given sleeping pills, not maps. We are told to ignore our dreams, not to navigate them with intention.

The Forgotten Map: What Are the Bardo States?

The concept of the "bardo" comes to us from Tibetan Buddhism, and it literally means an "intermediate state" or a "transitional state." While it is most famously associated with the state between death and rebirth, the teachings clarify that we are constantly moving through various bardos in life and in dream. These are gaps in continuity, moments where one reality dissolves and another has not yet solidified. The dream state is a bardo. The space between thoughts is a bardo. The moment you wake up, confused and disoriented, is a bardo. And yes, the journey after you draw your last breath is the ultimate bardo.

Understanding these states is not an academic exercise. It is a critical survival skill for the soul. The ancient wisdom traditions, like the teachings on the Vedantic dream states, provided us with a framework for understanding these non-ordinary states of consciousness. They mapped the terrain of the mind, not as a subject of clinical study, but as a living, breathing, sacred landscape. The Vedantic tradition identifies four states of consciousness: Vaishvanara (the waking state), Taijasa (the dreaming state), Prajna (deep, dreamless sleep), and Turiya (the fourth state, pure consciousness, which underlies the other three). Navigating the bardo of dreams is about learning to maintain the light of Turiya, the unwavering awareness of your true nature, even as the dreamscapes of Taijasa shift and morph around you.

You're here to become the silence that transforms the room.

James Hillman argued that we should not interpret dreams — we should let dreams interpret us. That distinction changes everything.

In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen countless souls arrive at the threshold of the bardo utterly terrified, their spiritual muscles atrophied from a lifetime of avoiding the beautiful, terrible truth of their own inner wilderness. They are terrified of the dissolution of self, the letting go of the familiar, because they have no anchor in the vastness of their own being. They cling to the wreckage of their waking identity, even when it is a prison. The dreamtime offers a nightly opportunity to practice this letting go, to become comfortable with the groundlessness of existence.

The Dreamtime as Bardo Training Ground

Every night, you are rehearsing for your death. This is not a morbid pronouncement; it is a statement of profound opportunity. The dream state mirrors the post-death bardo in its fluidity, its intensity, and its responsiveness to your own mind. The fears, attachments, and unresolved karmic patterns that haunt your dreams are the very same energies that will shape your journey after you leave this body. If you are chased by monsters in your dreams, you will be chased by the projections of your own terrified mind in the bardo. If you are lost and confused in your dreams, you will be lost and confused when the anchor of your physical body is gone.

To navigate the dream bardo is to cultivate what is known as "lucid awareness." This is not merely about controlling the dream, making it a playground for your ego. That is a childish fantasy. True lucidity is about recognizing the dream *as* a dream, and maintaining a stable, compassionate awareness within it. It is about meeting the terrifying figures not with fear, but with the understanding that they are empty projections of your own mind. It is about seeing the seductive, beautiful mirages and not being attached to them, knowing they too are ephemeral.

You're allowed to burn your 'nice' mask and replace it with nothing — no identity, no role, just the wild holy breath of your actual being.

This practice of lucid navigation is a radical act of self-liberation. It is a declaration that you will no longer be a passive victim of your own unconscious processes. You are claiming your sovereignty as the creator of your reality, both in the dreamtime and in the waking world. This is not about positive thinking or avoiding the darkness. It is about turning towards the darkness with the lamp of your awareness and discovering that it was never dark to begin with. It was only unseen.

May you see past the ordinary to the infinite. May you tell the truth even when it burns — especially to yourself.

Confronting the Gatekeepers: Your Own Mind

The guardians of the threshold, the terrifying deities and demonic figures described in the bardo teachings, are not external entities waiting to judge you. They are the personified energies of your own karma. They are the parts of yourself you have disowned, repressed, and projected onto others. The rage you have swallowed, the grief you have not allowed yourself to feel, the desires you have shamed into the shadows—they all await you in the bardo of dreams. And they will demand an accounting.

Your task is not to fight them. You cannot win a war against yourself. Your task is to recognize them. To see the face of the demon and say, "I know you. You are my own un-metabolized anger. I will no longer run from you." This is the moment of transformation. When you meet these energies with recognition and compassion, they lose their power over you. The demon transforms into a wisdom dakini. The terrifying monster becomes a guide. This is the alchemy of the dream bardo.

This is why the spiritual bypassers and the love-and-light crowd are so dangerous. They teach you to turn away from your own shadow, to cover your fear with affirmations and your rage with forced forgiveness. They are preparing you for a catastrophic failure in the bardo. They are teaching you to be a victim. For more on the importance of rigorous psychological inquiry, the American Psychological Association provides resources that underscore the necessity of facing, not avoiding, difficult mental and emotional content.

You weren't born to be lovable. You were born to be free.

If they've got a tattoo of a genderless inner child floating in a pink bubble… run.

Once you taste real freedom, nothing else will ever feel real again.

The Perils of Spiritual Tourism in the Dreamscape

There is a growing trend of what I call "spiritual tourism" in the dream world. People are using lucid dreaming techniques to create fantasy worlds, to indulge in escapism, to feed the very ego that the practice is meant to dissolve. They want the power without the responsibility, the light without the heat. They treat the dreamscape like a video game, a consequence-free zone for spiritual entertainment. This is a grave error.

The dream bardo is not a playground. It is a sacred space, a temple of the mind. To enter it with a frivolous or extractive intention is to desecrate it. It is to reinforce the very patterns of grasping and aversion that keep you trapped in the cycle of suffering. The point is not to become the master of a dream circus, but to become a humble student of your own consciousness. The point is to learn the art of surrender, of non-attachment, of fearless presence.

The energies you encounter in the dream bardo are real. The karmic imprints you create through your actions in the dream state are real. They will follow you back into the waking world. If you use the dreamtime to indulge in violence, greed, or lust, you are strengthening those seeds within your own mindstream. You are not escaping anything; you are digging your own prison deeper.

Practical Steps for Bardo Navigation

So how do we begin this formidable journey? It starts not with complex visualizations or esoteric rituals, but with the simple, earthy practice of paying attention. You must become a student of your own consciousness, a detective of your own inner world. This is not passive observation; it is an active, engaged inquiry.

First, keep a dream journal. And I don’t mean a few scribbled notes on your phone. I mean a dedicated, physical journal. The act of writing by hand forges a different, more intimate connection with the material. Write down everything you can remember, no matter how fragmented or bizarre. The emotions, the colors, the sensations. Over time, you will begin to see the patterns, the recurring symbols, the unresolved stories that your psyche is trying to process.

Second, as I mentioned, practice state checks during the day. This is crucial. The habit of questioning your reality must be ingrained in your waking life to have any hope of surfacing in your dream life. Don’t just ask the question; feel into the answer. Look at your hands. Read a line of text, look away, and read it again. In a dream, your hands will often appear distorted, and text will shift and change. These are anchors of lucidity.

Third, cultivate a relationship with your breath. Your breath is the bridge between the conscious and unconscious, the waking world and the dream world. Before sleep, lie on your back and simply follow your breath. Feel the rise and fall of your abdomen. This simple act of mindfulness can become a thread of awareness that you carry with you as you drift into the bardo of sleep.

The Gift Buried in The Bardo States

The ultimate gift of navigating the bardo states in your dreams is not a more entertaining nightlife. It is the profound and unshakable realization of your own immortal nature. When you can remain aware as the world dissolves around you, as your own identity melts away, you touch the deathless. You touch the part of you that was never born and will never die. This is the liberation that the great masters speak of. It is not a belief. It is a direct, lived experience.

This is not a journey for the timid. It will ask everything of you. It will demand that you confront every lie you have ever told yourself, every comfort you have clung to, every identity you have mistaken for your true self. It will burn you down to the ground. But from those ashes, something new can be born. Something real. Something free.

Seriously, this existence is just a complicated Flashmob — pretending that it's a forever deal. It's not.

The navigation of the dream bardo is the secret key, the hidden teaching that has been lost in the noise of the modern world. It is the path back to yourself. It is the ultimate preparation for the ultimate journey. Do not waste your nights in unconscious slumber. Do not waste your life in fearful avoidance. The gate is open. The path is clear. The choice, as always, is yours.

Frequently Asked Questions

How can I start to become more aware in my dreams?

Begin with intention. Before you sleep, make a firm resolution to remember your dreams and to recognize that you are dreaming. Throughout the day, practice "state checks"—ask yourself, "Am I dreaming right now?" and truly investigate your reality. This habit will carry over into the dream state, creating the initial spark of lucidity. Do not be discouraged by slow progress; you are undoing a lifetime of unconsciousness. This is a marathon, not a sprint.

Are the figures I meet in my dreams real?

They are as real as you are. They are living aspects of your own consciousness, fragmented and personified. To ask if they are "real" is to miss the point. The question is not their ontological status, but your relationship to them. Treat them with the respect you would give any sentient being, while holding the awareness that they are arising from the vast, empty ground of your own mind. In that paradox lies the key to their transformation, and yours.

The work is to BECOME LOVE IN EVERY MOMENT. And this takes gobs and gobs of personal work.

The work is not to escape the dream, but to awaken within it. To bring the full force of your love and your clarity to the darkest corners of your own psyche. This is the path of the spiritual warrior. It is a path of radical self-acceptance and fierce compassion. May you remember, on the hard days, that you are the embodiment of infinite love. May you walk it with courage and with an open heart, knowing that every step you take in the dreamtime is a step toward your own liberation.

faqs.question: Should I be concerned if these dreams are disturbing?
faqs.answer: Disturbing dreams are not the problem — they are the alarm system. The unconscious escalates intensity when gentler messages have been ignored. A disturbing dream is your psyche saying: this matters enough to make you uncomfortable.

faqs.question: Can two people share the same dream experience?
faqs.answer: Shared dreaming is reported across cultures and throughout history. Whether you frame it as telepathy, collective unconscious, or coincidence depends on your worldview. What matters is what the shared dream is communicating to each person individually.

faqs.question: How can I work with these dreams practically?
faqs.answer: Start by writing the dream down within five minutes of waking. Do not interpret — describe. Then sit with the images for a day before you try to make meaning. The dream will reveal itself on its own schedule, not yours.
